questions.question: Given the IPv4 address 192.168.10.50/26, determine: (i) Network address (ii) Broadcast address (iii) First usable host IP (iv) Last usable host IP (v) Total number of usable hosts.
keyPoints: /26 means subnet mask 255.255.255.192 (2 bits for host portion)

questions.question: Explain the IPv6 address notation rules. Convert the following compressed IPv6 address to its full form: 2001:0db8::1428:57ab.
keyPoints: IPv6 uses 128-bit addresses represented as 8 groups of 4 hexadecimal digits
Leading zeros in each group can be omitted
One consecutive group of zeros can be replaced by :: (only once)

questions.question: Draw and explain the IPv4 header structure. Also explain the significance of the following fields: TTL, Protocol, Fragment Offset, and Header Checksum. How does the Total Length field help in reassembly?
keyPoints: IPv4 header contains: Version, IHL, TOS, Total Length, Identification, Flags, Fragment Offset, TTL, Protocol, Header Checksum, Source IP, Destination IP, Options
TTL (Time to Live): Prevents packets from circulating indefinitely; decremented by each router
Protocol: Identifies the upper layer protocol (TCP=6, UDP=17, ICMP=1)
Fragment Offset: Indicates position of fragment in original datagram (13-bit field)

questions.question: Draw and explain the IPv6 header structure. Compare it with IPv4 header, highlighting at least 6 key differences. Also explain why IPv6 eliminates the need for fragmentation and checksum calculation at each router.
keyPoints: IPv6 header has 8 fields: Version, Traffic Class, Flow Label, Payload Length, Next Header, Hop Limit, Source Address, Destination Address
Key differences: 128-bit vs 32-bit addresses, simplified header (40 bytes vs 20-60 bytes), no Header Checksum, no Fragmentation at routers, Flow Label for QoS, Next Header instead of Protocol
IPv6 eliminates fragmentation by requiring path MTU discovery; routers don't fragment packets
IPv6 removes checksum to improve processing speed; link-layer CRC provides error detection
